did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning framework combining DenseNet201 and Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures for skin lesion classification. The model was trained on the ISIC skin cancer dataset using data augmentation, regularization, and adversarial training to achieve high accuracy in diagnosing skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a hybrid deep learning framework (DenseNet201 and ViT) applied to the ISIC dataset. It details specific methodology (data augmentation, adversarial training), reports new performance metrics (99.50% accuracy, 0.99 Kappa), and discusses limitations and future work, which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Improving Skin Lesion Diagnosis with a Hybrid Deep Learning Framework: A Study using ISIC
journal: 2025 International Conference on Quantum Photonics, Artificial Intelligence, and Networking (QPAIN)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning framework combining DenseNet201 and Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures to improve multiclass skin lesion classification. The model was trained and evaluated on the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, which contains 2,357 dermoscopic images across nine diagnostic categories. To enhance generalizability and robustness, the authors employed data augmentation, regularization, and adversarial training techniques during the experimental setup. The ensemble approach outperformed individual DenseNet201 and ViT models, achieving a validation accuracy of 99.50%, precision of 99.55%, recall of 99.52%, and an F1-score of 99.53%. Additionally, the model demonstrated high consistency with a Cohen’s Kappa coefficient of 0.99. These results highlight the potential of the proposed ensemble framework for reliable, high-accuracy clinical skin cancer diagnostics.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the nine diagnostic categories in the ISIC dataset used for multiclass skin lesion classification.

The paper states the ISIC dataset contains nine categories including melanoma, nevus, and basal cell carcinoma, and mentions 'others' but does not explicitly list the remaining six specific lesion types.

all_task_types.target: Nine diagnostic categories of skin lesions (including melanoma, nevus, basal cell carcinoma, and others)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'multiclass skin lesion classification' using the ISIC dataset with nine classes. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using a hybrid DenseNet201 and ViT architecture with a soft voting strategy. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Cohen's Kappa.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions, annotated according to ISIC standards. It was sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive.

use.training: The dataset was stratified into training and testing portions at an 80:20 ratio. Data augmentation and stratified sampling were used to address class imbalance.
use.validation: The model achieved validation metrics (99.50% accuracy, etc.) on the dataset, implying a validation split was used, though the specific count is not reported.
use.testing: The dataset was divided into balanced training and testing subsets. The 20% testing portion was used for final evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the consistency and predictive agreement between the model's predicted outputs and the actual ground-truth labels.
To address class imbalance and ensure balanced representation of classes in the training and testing subsets.
To combine predicted probabilities from the DenseNet201 and ViT models in the ensemble framework.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, Cohen’s Kappa) and comparative analysis against baseline models without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset.experiment: Comparison of DenseNet201, ViT, and Ensemble models
ISIC Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: The ensemble model outperformed individual DenseNet201 and ViT models in validation accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and Kappa, while achieving the lowest validation loss. The study notes that the ensemble exhibited more stable learning dynamics and better generalization. Adversarial training was incorporated, and robustness was assessed using PGD and FGSM attacks, though specific quantitative robustness metrics were not reported in the provided text.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Deep Learning Framework combining DenseNet201 and Vision Transformer (ViT)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning and fine-tuning were used for both DenseNet201 and ViT architectures.

dense_net_details.blocks: Dense Blocks with 1x1 and 3x3 convolutional layers
dense_net_details.transitions: Transition blocks with 1x1 convolution and 2x2 average pooling
custom_head: Output Dense layer (9 units with Softmax)

vit_details.embedding: Linear mapping of patches to embeddings with positional encodings
vit_details.normalization: Layer normalization applied around attention components
vit_details.classification_token: Class Token (CLS) used for final decision

architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of feature vectors from DenseNet201 and ViT, followed by a dense layer with softmax activation

training_details.regularization: Dropout (0.5) in DenseNet head; general regularization techniques mentioned

classification_or_output_head.fusion_layer: Dense layer with softmax activation applied to concatenated features

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of intensity values to the [0, 1] range
Data augmentation including random rotation (0-360 degrees), zoom (20%), horizontal/vertical flipping, brightness adjustment (±25%), and contrast normalization
Stratified sampling to address class imbalance
Adversarial training to improve model resilience

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a range of 0-360 degrees.
Applied with a zoom factor of 20%.
Applied to images to increase training set variation.
Incorporated during the development phase to improve model resilience against adversarial attacks (PGD and FGSM).

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning framework combining DenseNet201 and Vision Transformer (ViT) architectures outperformed individual DenseNet201 and ViT models in skin lesion classification.
The ensemble model achieved high validation performance metrics: 99.50% accuracy, 99.55% precision, 99.52% recall, 99.53% F1-score, and a Cohen’s Kappa coefficient of 0.99.
Integrating local features from CNNs with global features from Transformers enhanced classification metrics, robustness, and generalization compared to using either architecture alone.
The model demonstrated stable learning dynamics and better generalization, with validation curves closely following training trends, indicating minimal overfitting.
The study acknowledges that the lack of interpretability in the opaque ensemble model constrains clinical trust and adoption.
The ensemble approach demands substantial computational power due to dual-model inference and feature fusion, which may hinder deployment in resource-constrained environments.

The reliance on the ISIC dataset, which may lack sufficient representation of demographic and imaging variability, limits the model's ability to generalize effectively to real-world clinical settings.

Future work will focus on integrating Explainable AI (XAI) methods, exploring domain adaptation to bridge gaps between ISIC and clinical data, and investigating self-supervised and few-shot learning strategies.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC dataset (2,357 images), which the authors acknowledge may lack sufficient demographic and imaging variability, limiting generalizability to real-world clinical settings.
The proposed ensemble model is described as 'opaque' and lacks interpretability, which the authors identify as a significant barrier to clinical trust and adoption.
The model demands substantial computational power due to dual-model inference (DenseNet201 and ViT) and feature fusion, potentially hindering deployment in resource-constrained environments.
Performance metrics are exceptionally high (99.50% validation accuracy, 0.99 Kappa), which may indicate overfitting or dataset-specific bias despite the use of data augmentation and regularization.
No formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; comparisons between models were based on descriptive metrics.
Adversarial robustness was assessed using PGD and FGSM attacks, but specific quantitative robustness metrics were not reported in the provided text.
The paper is a conference publication (QPAIN 2025), which may have less rigorous peer review compared to journal publications.

The dataset split was 80:20 for training and testing, but the specific number of images per class was not reported, raising potential concerns about class imbalance handling despite the use of stratified sampling.
